content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

June 20, 1990


D. William Heine
Schwartz, Steinsapir, Dohrmann & Sommers
3580 Wilshire Boulevard, Suite 1820
Los Angeles, CA 90010-2594

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-90-370

Dear Mr. Heine:

This is in response to your request for advice on behalf of
the Democratic Agenda For the 90's (the "Agenda") with respect to
the contribution limitations of the Political Reform Act (the
"Act").¹

This letter represents conservative advice which will be
submitted to the Commission for review at a future meeting.

QUESTION

Can the Agenda lawfully transfer money which has been brought
into compliance with the contribution limits of the Act from its
non-candidate account to its candidate account, and then use that
money to make contributions to candidates?

CONCLUSION

Funds received in excess of the contribution limits and
placed in an account to be used for purposes other than making
contributions to candidates cannot be divided at some later time
into contributions to candidate and non-candidate accounts.

FACTS

The Agenda, a recipient committee, has an account which has
been used to collect and disburse money for use in voter registra-
tion and get-out-the-vote drives, but not to make contributions to
candidates (the "non-candidate account"). The Agenda is in the

________________________
1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

File No. A-90-370
Page 2


process of establishing another account which will be used to make
contributions to candidates (the "candidate account").

You wish to know if funds in the non-candidate account may be
transferred to the candidate account, provided the transferred
money is brought into conformity with the fiscal year contribution
limits of the Act. You propose the following procedure to accomplish this:

1. The Agenda will establish a separate account for transfer
of contributions deemed in compliance with the contribution limits
of the Act.

2. The Agenda will presume that the total amount of cash and
cash equivalents on hand in its non-candidate account when the
separate bank account is established consists of contributions
most recently received. Loans receivable shall be included in the
total of cash and cash equivalents.

3. The Agenda will review contributions received by the non-
candidate account beginning with the last contribution received
and working back in time until the total amount of cash and cash
equivalents on hand is reached. Should the review indicate that
any contributions to the non-candidate account exceed the applicable limitations set forth in the Act, the total amount of
cash and cash equivalents that will be deposited in an allocated
to the candidate account will be reduced by the amount in excess
of the applicable contribution limits.

4. In determining whether a contribution is in compliance
with the limits, the Agenda shall aggregate the total amount of
contributions received in the fiscal year from each person,
political committee, broad-based political committee, and
political party.

5. The contributions which are brought into compliance with
the limits will be deposited in the account for use in making
contributions to candidates. All Agenda contributions to
candidates will comply with Sections 85303 and 85305 of the Act
and the regulations promulgated thereunder.

6. The Agenda will report on its campaign statements all
contributions deposited in and expenditures made from the
candidate account.

ANALYSIS

The Act limits the amount that any person may contribute to a
committee for the purpose of making contributions to candidates,
as follows:

No person shall make and no political committee,
broad based political committee, or political party
shall solicit or accept, any contribution or loan from a

File No. A-90-370
Page 3


person which would cause the total amount contributed or
loaned by that person to the same political committee,
broad based political committee, or political party to
exceed two thousand five hundred dollars ($2,500) in any
fiscal year to make contributions to candidates for
elective office.

Section 85302, emphasis added.

The Act further provides that the limitation on contributions
to political committees does not apply to "financial or other sup-
port to one or more political committees or broad based political
committees provided the support is used for purposes other than
making contributions directly to candidates for elective office."
(Section 85303(c), emphasis added.)

Based upon the foregoing, we have advised that contributions
to political committees are not subject to the contribution
limits, provided the contributions are not for the purpose of mak-
ing contributions to candidates. (Calvert Advise Letter, No.
A-089-403, copy enclosed.) However, we have previously advised
that this applies only when the contributions received by the
committee are not commingled with funds used to make contributions
to candidates. (Dowell Advice Letter, No. A-89-566, copy
enclosed.)

In December of 1989, the Commission adopted Regulation 185352
(copy enclosed), which interpreted Section 85303(c) and outlined
the circumstances permitting contributions in excess of the
limits. That regulation provides that contributions received by a
political committee in excess of the contribution limits must be
deposited in a separate account which does not include funds used
to make contributions to candidates. (Regulation 18535(a)(2).)
The funds in that account may be expended only for purposes other
than to make contributions to candidates for elective office.
(Regulation 18535(a)(3).) A contribution received in excess of
the limits must be returned unless the portion in excess of the
contribution limits has been earmarked for use other than making
contributions to candidates either in a written solicitation for
contributions by the political committee or in a written document
received from the contributor at or near the time of the contribu-
tion. (Regulation 18535(c).)

According to the facts stated in your letter, the funds which
you propose to segregate into a candidate account are presently
"commingled" with funds received in excess of the limits. You do
not indicate that there is any written record establishing the
contributor's intent that the funds be allocated between a
candidate and non-candidate account. Moreover, since the making

2    Regulation 18535 is in the rulemaking process. Although not
currently effective, the regulation expresses the policy of the

File No. A-90-370
Page 4


or solicitation of a contribution in excess of the limits for
purposes of making contributions to candidates would violate the
Act, it is more likely than not that there was either an express
or implied understanding, at the time a contribution in excess of
the limits was solicited and made, that it would not be used to
make contributions to candidates.

Section 85303(c) represents an exception to the general
thrust of Proposition 73 to limit campaign contributions and
should be narrowly construed. It simply prohibits using the
contribution limits imposed by Proposition 73 as a person's
ability to provide financial support to a political committee for
purposes other than to make contributions to candidates. Nothing
in section provides for use of some portion of such financial support to make contributions to candidates.

The present policy adopted by the Commission requiring written identification and segregation of funds between candidate and
non-candidate accounts at or near the time of receipt represents a
narrow construction of Section 85303(c). It permits a person to
make a contribution within the limits to a political committee for
use in making contributions to candidates and to contribute excess
funds for purposes other than making contributions to candidates.
However, funds received in excess of the limits and commingled in
an account which is used for purposes other than making contributions to candidates may not be used to make contributions to
candidates. Therefore, we would conservatively advise that funds
received in excess of the contribution limits and placed in an
account to be used for purposes other than making contributions to
candidates cannot be divided into contributions to candidate and non-candidate accounts.

As indicated above, this letter represents conservative
advice which will be submitted to the Commission for review.
Should the Commission adopt a broader interpretation of Section
85303(c) which would permit the segregation of funds, we believe
that the method proposed by you for accomplishing the allocation
to the candidate account is a reasonable one. However, one
additional step would be required in order to assure compliance with
the Act. Section 85304 prohibits transfers of funds between
candidates or their controlled committees. This has been
interpreted as prohibiting candidates from indirectly transferring
contributions to other candidates by making contributions to
political committees for the purpose of making contributions to
candidates. (Calvert Advice Letter, supra.) Therefore, any funds
included in the funds subject to transfer to the candidate account
must be reduced by any amount which represents a contribution to
the committee from a candidate.

File No. A-90-370
Page 5


     If you have any questions regarding the above, you may
contact me at (916) 322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,


                                        Scott Hallabrin
                                        Acting General Counsel
                                        [signature]

                                        By:  Margaret W. Ellison
                                             Counsel, Legal Division

Enclosure
